one_line_takeaway: An open-source, commercial-grade prompt-injection-robust LLM (Meta SecAlign 8B/70B) plus the SecAlign++ DPO recipe—randomized injection position + self-generated labels—that near-eliminates static PI ASR and generalizes to unseen agentic tasks, though it remains vulnerable to white-box GCG attacks (~47% ASR).
key_finding: META-SECALIGN-70B, though trained only on generic instruction-tuning samples, generalizes its PI robustness to unseen agentic tool-calling and web-navigation tasks and reaches a utility-security frontier comparable to GPT-5 and more secure than several flagship proprietary defended models. Reported own results: 6.4% ASR on SEP, 1.9% on AgentDojo, 0% on WASP End2End; InjecAgent ASR falls from 53.8% (undefended) to 0.5%, AgentDojo from 14.7% to 1.9%, with only ~2% max utility drop on general-knowledge benchmarks. Both novel techniques matter: randomized injection position lifts AgentDojo utility from 15.5% to 84.5% without hurting security, and self-generated responses beat GPT-5-annotated labels on security (e.g., SEP Basic Adaptive ASR 6.4% vs 21.3%). SecAlign++ also dominates SecAlign under adaptive attacks (70B SEP Basic Adaptive 6.4% vs 18.9%).
core_contribution: The paper releases META SECALIGN (8B and 70B), described as the first fully open-source LLM with a built-in model-level defense against prompt injection (PI) that reaches commercial-grade utility and is usable for agentic tasks. The core technical contribution is SecAlign++, a defensive fine-tuning recipe built on top of the prior SecAlign DPO defense with two additions the authors themselves introduce: (1) randomized injection position (moving ~half of simulated injections to the start of the untrusted-data segment rather than always the end, to prevent the model learning a 'ignore the last sentence' shortcut), and (2) self-generated responses (using the undefended initialization model as the annotator to produce in-distribution desirable/undesirable DPO responses, instead of the low-quality TEXT_DAVINCI_003 labels in the public Alpaca dataset). The recipe adds a new 'input' message role to the chat template to separate untrusted data from trusted system/user prompts. Note: the base SecAlign DPO formulation and the StruQ input-separation idea are cited prior work, not this paper's invention; SecAlign++ is the delta.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection against LLM-integrated applications. The system, user, and LLM provider are benign; the environment (external data retrieved via tools, web pages, documents) is malicious and carries an injected instruction. The attacker knows the benign prompt and the LLM's prompt template but cannot modify them. This excludes jailbreaks and direct/system-message-following attacks where a malicious user supplies the instruction. The paper also evaluates adaptive attackers with LLM-embedding access (fake-delimiter 'escape' attacks) and a white-box GCG suffix attacker.
limitations: Against the strongest white-box GCG adaptive attack, robustness is far weaker: 47.3% AlpacaFarm GCG Adaptive ASR on the 70B model (better than SecAlign's 53.9% but still high), showing the defense is not robust to gradient-based attackers with full access. Commercial-model comparisons are explicitly 'non-apples-to-apples' (different training data/recipes; closed-source models forced through a dummy-tool channel for data). The authors cannot explain why AgentDojo utility increases after fine-tuning for Llama-3.3-70B (does not occur for other models). The scaling claim ('stronger LLMs are more vulnerable if undefended') rests on a proxy study assuming same-series models share post-training recipes, not a controlled experiment. Defense assumes the model's special delimiters can be recursively filtered from untrusted data; embedding-space fake-delimiter attacks partly circumvent this.

datasets_used: Cleaned-Alpaca instruction-tuning dataset (training; 19,157-sample DPO preference set)
MMLU, MMLU-Pro, IFEval, BBH, GPQA Diamond (general-knowledge utility)
AlpacaFarm / AlpacaEval2 (instruction-following utility and ASR; 805 samples, 208 with data)
quantitative_results: META-SECALIGN-70B ASR: SEP 6.4%, AgentDojo 1.9%, WASP End2End 0%, InjecAgent 0.5%, TaskTracker 0.2%, CyberSecEval2 1.8%, AlpacaFarm 0.5%
InjecAgent ASR: 53.8% undefended -> 0.5% defended; AgentDojo ASR: 14.7% -> 1.9%; CyberSecEval2: 52.7% -> 1.8%
Randomized injection position (70B, Table 1): AgentDojo utility 15.5% -> 84.5%; AgentDojo utility-with-attack 14.9% -> 79.5%; security unchanged/improved (AgentDojo ASR 0% -> 1.9%)
Self-generated (SELF) vs GPT-5 labels (70B, Table 2): SEP Basic Adaptive ASR 6.4% (SELF) vs 21.3% (GPT-4O) vs 97.8% (GPT-5); CyberSecEval2 1.8% (SELF) vs 36.4% (GPT-5)
SecAlign++ vs SecAlign (70B, Table 6): SEP Basic Adaptive ASR 6.4% vs 18.9%; AlpacaFarm Basic Adaptive 0.5% vs 8.2%; GCG Adaptive 47.3% vs 53.9%; SEP utility 60.4% vs 51.6%
General-knowledge utility drop (70B, Table 3): MMLU 86.3%->85.9%, IFEval 91.3%->89.5%, GPQA Diamond 50.0%->48.0% (max ~2%)
Generality (Table 7): QWEN3-4B AlpacaFarm ASR 100%->1.0% (MMLU 70.7%->70.6%); LLAMA-4-SCOUT AlpacaFarm ASR 87.0%->3.4% (MMLU 85.9%->85.3%)
70B trained ~7h on 8x H200; 8B ~0.5h on 8x H100; weights downloaded 16K times
baselines_compared: SecAlign (prior SoTA model-level defense; direct ablation baseline)
Alternative DPO annotators: TEXT_DAVINCI_003, GPT-4O, GPT-5 (label-source ablation)
